title: Stranger Cole

infobox.value: Wilburn Theodore Cole   (1942-06-26) 26 June 1942 (age 83)  [Kingston, Jamaica](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica")

(born **Wilburn Theodore Cole**, 26 June 1942)[[1]](#cite_note-Thompson-1) is a [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") singer whose long recording career dates from the early days of [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") in 1962 through to the present.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Musical artist

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Stranger_Cole_%26_The_Steadytones_Rudolstadt_2024_29.jpg/250px-Stranger_Cole_%26_The_Steadytones_Rudolstadt_2024_29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stranger_Cole_%26_The_Steadytones_Rudolstadt_2024_29.jpg)

Stranger Cole & The Steadytones performing in Rudolstadt, 2024

**Stranger Cole**, also known as **StrangeJah Cole** (born **Wilburn Theodore Cole**, 26 June 1942)[[1]](#cite_note-Thompson-1) is a [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") singer whose long recording career dates from the early days of [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") in 1962 through to the present.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stranger_Cole&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Cole was born in [Kingston, Jamaica](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica") on 26 July 1942 and nicknamed "Stranger" by his family, as they considered that he did not resemble any member of his family.[[1]](#cite_note-Thompson-1)

Cole was initially successful as a songwriter, writing "In and out the Window", which was a hit for Eric "Monty" Morris.[[4]](#cite_note-Boehm-4) This success gave him the chance to make his recording debut in 1962, instantly finding success with singles such as "Rough and Tough" and "When You Call My Name" (a duet with Patsy Todd) for producer [Arthur "Duke" Reid](/wiki/Duke_Reid "Duke Reid").[[5]](#cite_note-Larkin-5) Further success followed with singles for Reid through to the mid-1960s, and he also worked with other producers at this time, including [Clement "Coxsone" Dodd](/wiki/Clement_%22Coxsone%22_Dodd "Clement \"Coxsone\" Dodd") (a duet with [Ken Boothe](/wiki/Ken_Boothe "Ken Boothe") on "Worlds Fair"), and [Prince Buster](/wiki/Prince_Buster "Prince Buster").[[5]](#cite_note-Larkin-5) Further duets included recordings with [Gladstone Anderson](/wiki/Gladstone_Anderson "Gladstone Anderson") (on "Just Like a River")[[6]](#cite_note-Leggett-6) and [Hortense Ellis](/wiki/Hortense_Ellis "Hortense Ellis"), the tendency to record duets apparently due to his shyness when it came to singing alone.[[1]](#cite_note-Thompson-1) In the late 1960s and early 1970s he recorded with several producers, including [Bunny Lee](/wiki/Bunny_Lee "Bunny Lee"), [Lee "Scratch" Perry](/wiki/Lee_%22Scratch%22_Perry "Lee \"Scratch\" Perry"), and [Sonia Pottinger](/wiki/Sonia_Pottinger "Sonia Pottinger").[[7]](#cite_note-Moskowitz-7) These included further material with Todd as "Stranger & Patsy."

In 1971 Cole emigrated to the United Kingdom, where he toured extensively, and moved on again to Canada in 1973, settling in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto").[[5]](#cite_note-Larkin-5)[[8]](#cite_note-8) He worked as a machinist in the [Tonka Toy](/wiki/Tonka "Tonka") factory in Toronto and later opened a record store, the first Caribbean shop in Toronto's [Kensington Market](/wiki/Kensington_Market "Kensington Market") area. His first album, *"Forward" in the Land of Sunshine*, was released in 1976, with a handful of further albums released over the next ten years, mostly on his own label.[[5]](#cite_note-Larkin-5) In 2006, Cole released his first album in twenty years, *Morning Train*, a collaboration with [Jah Shaka](/wiki/Jah_Shaka "Jah Shaka"). Cole is featured in the 2009 documentary *Rocksteady: The Roots of Reggae*, in which he and other stars of the rocksteady era reunited to record a new album of the same name, released in August 2009.[[9]](#cite_note-Braun-9) Cole's sons, Squiddly and Marcus, followed him into a music career: Squiddly working as a [drummer](/wiki/Drummer "Drummer") for artists including [Ziggy Marley](/wiki/Ziggy_Marley "Ziggy Marley") and [Mutabaruka](/wiki/Mutabaruka "Mutabaruka");[[7]](#cite_note-Moskowitz-7) and Marcus Cole (aka KxritoXisen) producing music for his father.

## "Bangarang"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stranger_Cole&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: \"Bangarang\"")]

Stranger Cole is credited with creating the first reggae song with his hit, "Bangarang".[[10]](#cite_note-10) The song was recorded in 1968 at Duke Reid's Studio with sound engineer Bunny 'Striker' Lee where Cole performed the song with saxophonist Lester Sterling and keyboard player Lloyd Charmers.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Discography

* *The First Ten Years* (1978), Various Artists, Camelot
* *Capture Land* (1978), with Chalawa, Green Weenie
* *The Patriot* (1982), Stranger Cole
* *No More Fussing and Fighting* (1986), Scorcher
* *Dramatic* (2003), Stranger Cole & King Banana

* *Hice Gold*, Wacam
